Clean Hands, Healthy Lives: The Global Healthcare Hygiene Crisis

A UNICEF Data Report on Health Facility Sanitation

Author

Vaishnav Venugopal Nedungadi

Published

April 14, 2026

Code 
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
Mounted at /content/drive

1 Introduction

Imagine visiting a hospital or clinic expecting care — only to find it lacks basic cleaning services. No soap. No clean water. No sanitation. This is not a rare edge case: it is the daily reality for millions of people across the developing world.

This report analyses UNICEF data on the proportion of health care facilities with no cleaning service, a critical yet underreported indicator of global health infrastructure. When a health facility cannot maintain basic hygiene, it becomes a place that spreads disease rather than preventing it.

“Clean water, sanitation, and hygiene are the foundations of health. Without them, even the best medical staff cannot protect patients from infection.”

The consequences are severe:

  • Increased risk of healthcare-associated infections (HAIs)
  • Deterrence of patients who avoid facilities they distrust
  • Disproportionate impact on women giving birth and newborns
  • Undermining of vaccination campaigns and treatment programmes

Using UNICEF’s indicator data combined with World Bank economic and demographic metadata, this report maps the crisis, tracks trends over time, and examines how a country’s wealth and population size relate to its health facility cleanliness.

Code 
import polars as pl
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes, geom_col, geom_point, geom_line, geom_smooth,
    geom_map, labs, theme, theme_minimal, theme_bw, theme_classic,
    element_text, element_blank, element_rect, element_line,
    scale_fill_gradient, scale_fill_manual, scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_continuous, scale_y_continuous,
    coord_flip, guides, guide_legend,
    facet_wrap
)
import geopandas as gpd
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

print("Libraries loaded successfully.")
Libraries loaded successfully.

2 Data Loading and Exploration

We load three datasets provided by UNICEF:

  1. unicef_indicator_1.csv — Proportion of health care facilities with no cleaning service (our primary indicator)
  2. unicef_indicator_2.csv — HIV testing coverage for infants (supplementary indicator)
  3. unicef_metadata.csv — Country-level economic and demographic metadata (World Bank indicators)

We join these datasets on country codes to enrich our analysis with GDP, population, and life expectancy data.

Code 
# Load datasets using polars
indicator1 = pl.read_csv(
    "/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/unicef_indicator_1.csv"
)

indicator2 = pl.read_csv(
    "/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/unicef_indicator_2.csv"
)

metadata = pl.read_csv(
    "/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/unicef_metadata.csv"
)

# Basic data inspection
print(f"Indicator 1 shape: {indicator1.shape}")
print(f"Indicator 2 shape: {indicator2.shape}")
print(f"Metadata shape: {metadata.shape}")

print("\nIndicator 1 columns:", indicator1.columns)
print("\nMetadata columns:", metadata.columns)
Indicator 1 shape: (458, 14)
Indicator 2 shape: (970, 14)
Metadata shape: (13715, 15)

Indicator 1 columns: ['country', 'alpha_2_code', 'alpha_3_code', 'numeric_code', 'indicator', 'time_period', 'obs_value', 'sex', 'unit_multiplier', 'unit_of_measure', 'observation_status', 'observation_confidentaility', 'time_period_activity_related_to_when_the_data_are_collected', 'current_age']

Metadata columns: ['country', 'alpha_2_code', 'alpha_3_code', 'numeric_code', 'year', 'Population, total', 'GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)', 'GNI (current US$)', 'Inflation, consumer prices (annual %)', 'Life expectancy at birth, total (years)', 'Military expenditure (% of GDP)', 'Fossil fuel energy consumption (% of total)', 'GDP growth (annual %)', 'Birth rate, crude (per 1,000 people)', 'Hospital beds (per 1,000 people)']

print(indicator1.describe())

print(indicator1.select(pl.col("country")).unique().sort("country"))

print("\n=== Year range ===")
print(indicator1.select(pl.col("time_period").min().alias("min_year"),
                        pl.col("time_period").max().alias("max_year")))

Code 
# ── 1. Filter indicator 1 to 'Total' sex (avoid double-counting)
ind1_total = (
    indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .select(
        pl.col("country"),
        pl.col("alpha_3_code"),
        pl.col("time_period").alias("year"),
        pl.col("obs_value").alias("pct_no_cleaning")
    )
)

# ── 2. Compute most-recent value per country for the world map
latest_by_country = (
    ind1_total
    .group_by(pl.col("country"), pl.col("alpha_3_code"))
    .agg(
        pl.col("pct_no_cleaning").last().alias("latest_pct"),
        pl.col("year").max().alias("latest_year")
    )
    .sort("latest_pct", descending=True)
)

print("Latest data per country:")
print(latest_by_country.head(10))

# ── 3. Join indicator 1 with metadata for scatterplot
metadata_clean = (
    metadata
    .select(
        pl.col("alpha_3_code"),
        pl.col("year"),
        pl.col("GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)").alias("gdp_per_capita"),
        pl.col("Population, total").alias("population"),
        pl.col("Life expectancy at birth, total (years)").alias("life_expectancy")
    )
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_per_capita").is_not_null())
)

# Join on alpha_3_code and year
merged = (
    ind1_total
    .join(
        metadata_clean,
        on=["alpha_3_code", "year"],
        how="inner"
    )
    .filter(pl.col("pct_no_cleaning").is_not_null())
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_per_capita").is_not_null())
)

print(f"\nMerged dataset rows: {merged.shape[0]}")

# ── 4. Average per year for time-series chart
avg_per_year = (
    ind1_total
    .group_by(pl.col("year"))
    .agg(pl.col("pct_no_cleaning").mean().alias("avg_pct"))
    .sort("year")
)

# ── 5. Bar chart data – top 15 worst countries (latest value)
top15 = latest_by_country.head(15)

print("\nTop 15 countries by % facilities with no cleaning:")
print(top15)

3 The Global Picture

3.1 A World Map of Health Facility Hygiene

The map below reveals a stark geographic divide. The highest proportions of health facilities without cleaning services are concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South and Southeast Asia — precisely the regions where infectious disease burden is already highest and where clean clinical environments matter most.

Countries like Somalia, Niger, and Sudan stand out with more than half of their facilities lacking any cleaning service. This is not merely an inconvenience — it is a public health emergency that compounds every other health challenge these countries face.

Code 
import geopandas as gpd # Import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
import pandas as pd # Still needed for .to_pandas() and pl.from_pandas
import polars as pl # Ensure polars is imported for from_pandas

#| label: fig-world-map
#| fig-cap: "Proportion of health care facilities with no cleaning service (latest available year per country). Darker red indicates higher proportions."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| warning: false

# Load world geometries from the manually downloaded and uploaded shapefile
world = gpd.read_file("/content/drive/MyDrive/Colab Notebooks/ne_110m_admin_0_countries/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.shp")

# Convert polars to pandas for geopandas merge
latest_pd = latest_by_country.to_pandas()

# Merge world shapes with UNICEF data on ISO alpha-3 codes
world_merged = world.merge(
    latest_pd[["alpha_3_code", "latest_pct"]],
    left_on="ADM0_A3", # Changed 'iso_a3' to 'ADM0_A3'
    right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

# Convert back to polars-compatible format for plotnine
# (Note: world_plot is not directly used by the matplotlib plot, but kept for consistency if needed later)
# world_plot = pl.from_pandas(
#     world_merged[["NAME", "ADM0_A3", "latest_pct", "geometry"]] # Changed 'name' to 'NAME' (capitalized) to match Natural Earth shapefile column
#     .assign(geometry=world_merged["geometry"])
# )

# Use geopandas + matplotlib for the world map since plotnine geom_map needs GeoDataFrame
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(14, 7))

# Plot all countries in light grey first
world.plot(ax=ax, color="#e8e8e8", edgecolor="#cccccc", linewidth=0.4)

# Plot countries with data using a red colour scale
world_merged_data = world_merged.dropna(subset=["latest_pct"])
world_merged_data.plot(
    column="latest_pct",
    ax=ax,
    cmap="YlOrRd",
    edgecolor="#999999",
    linewidth=0.4,
    legend=True,
    legend_kwds={
        "label": "% Facilities with No Cleaning Service",
        "orientation": "horizontal",
        "shrink": 0.5,
        "pad": 0.02
    }
)

ax.set_title(
    "Health Care Facilities Without Any Cleaning Service",
    fontsize=16, fontweight="bold", pad=12
)
ax.set_axis_off()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("map_output.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
print("World map rendered.")
World map rendered.

3.2 The Worst Offenders: Country Rankings

The bar chart below ranks the 15 countries with the highest percentage of health facilities lacking cleaning services. The results are striking: several countries exceed 50% of all facilities — meaning that, on average, every other clinic or hospital has no cleaning service whatsoever.

This is not just a hygiene issue. It reflects systemic underfunding of health infrastructure, shortage of trained cleaning staff, and inadequate supply chains for basic cleaning materials like soap and disinfectant.

Code 
# Prepare data: sort and convert for plotnine
top15_pd = top15.to_pandas()
top15_pd = top15_pd.sort_values("latest_pct", ascending=True)
top15_pl = pl.from_pandas(top15_pd)

(
    ggplot(
        top15_pl.to_pandas(),
        aes(x="country", y="latest_pct", fill="latest_pct")
    )
    + geom_col()
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#f7c59f", high="#c0392b")
    + labs(
        title="Countries with Highest % of Health Facilities\nLacking Cleaning Services",
        x="",
        y="% of Facilities with No Cleaning Service",
        fill="%"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(face="bold", size=13),
        axis_text_y=element_text(size=10),
        axis_text_x=element_text(size=9),
        figure_size=(10, 6)
    )
)

4 The Wealth Connection

4.1 Does GDP Predict Facility Hygiene?

A critical question is whether this crisis is purely a resource problem — i.e., do richer countries simply have cleaner facilities? The scatterplot below explores the relationship between GDP per capita and the proportion of facilities lacking cleaning services.

The linear regression line confirms a strong negative relationship: as GDP per capita increases, the proportion of uncleaned facilities drops sharply. However, this is not a perfect relationship — some relatively wealthier countries still show high proportions, pointing to issues of governance, prioritisation, and health system management beyond raw wealth.

Code 
merged_pd = merged.to_pandas()

(
    ggplot(
        merged_pd,
        aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="pct_no_cleaning")
    )
    + geom_point(alpha=0.5, color="#e74c3c", size=2.5)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#2980b9", fill="#aed6f1", size=1.2)
    + scale_x_continuous(
        labels=lambda lst: [f"${int(x/1000)}k" for x in lst]
    )
    + labs(
        title="GDP Per Capita vs. Health Facility Hygiene",
        subtitle="Wealthier countries tend to have cleaner health facilities, but exceptions exist",
        x="GDP Per Capita (constant 2015 USD)",
        y="% Facilities with No Cleaning Service"
    )
    + theme_bw()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(face="bold", size=13),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(color="#555555", size=10),
        figure_size=(10, 6)
    )
)

5 Tracking Change Over Time

5.1 Is the Situation Improving?

The time-series chart below tracks the average percentage of health facilities without cleaning services across all countries in the dataset from 2000 to the most recent available year.

The trend tells a complex story. While there has been some improvement in certain periods — likely coinciding with global health initiatives and increased international aid — progress is neither linear nor consistent. The data reveals periods of stagnation and even deterioration, especially in years marked by conflict, economic crisis, or pandemic disruption.

The COVID-19 pandemic after 2020 likely placed additional strain on already-stretched health infrastructure, diverting resources away from routine maintenance and cleaning services.

Code 
avg_pd = avg_per_year.to_pandas()

(
    ggplot(
        avg_pd,
        aes(x="year", y="avg_pct")
    )
    + geom_line(color="#e74c3c", size=1.2)
    + geom_point(color="#c0392b", size=3)
    + scale_x_continuous(
        breaks=list(range(2000, 2024, 2))
    )
    + labs(
        title="Trend: Health Facilities Without Cleaning Services (2000–2023)",
        subtitle="Average across countries with available data each year",
        x="Year",
        y="Average % Facilities with No Cleaning Service"
    )
    + theme_classic()
    + theme(
        plot_title=element_text(face="bold", size=13),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(color="#555555", size=10),
        axis_text_x=element_text(angle=45, hjust=1),
        figure_size=(10, 5)
    )
)

6 Conclusions and Call to Action

This report has presented four complementary views of a single, critical global health challenge: the lack of cleaning services in health facilities across the developing world.

6.1 Key Findings

  • Geographic concentration: The crisis is most acute in Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of Asia, where the need for health services is already greatest.
  • Scale of the problem: In the worst-affected countries, more than half of all health facilities operate without any cleaning service — a figure that should alarm any public health professional.
  • The wealth factor: There is a clear relationship between national income and facility hygiene, but money alone is not the full answer. Governance, prioritisation, and political will all play a role.
  • Stalled progress: Despite decades of global health investment, progress has been slow and uneven. The situation in many countries has not significantly improved since 2000.

6.2 What Must Be Done

Achieving universal health coverage — one of the UN Sustainable Development Goals — is impossible without clean health facilities. The following actions are urgently needed:

  1. Increased investment in health facility infrastructure, specifically sanitation and hygiene services
  2. National hygiene standards with independent monitoring and accountability mechanisms
  3. Training and employment of dedicated health facility cleaners as part of the health workforce
  4. International partnerships to supply cleaning materials, especially in fragile and conflict-affected states
  5. Data collection improvements so that more countries can be tracked and held accountable

Clean facilities save lives. This is not a luxury — it is a fundamental right for every patient, everywhere.

Data sources: UNICEF Global Databases; World Bank Development Indicators. Analysis conducted using Python (Polars, plotnine). Report generated with Quarto.
